metadata.title: Why the Simulation That Took 8 Minutes to Generate Wasn't Theirs
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Claude Code for Teachers candidate 08. The mechanism: a one-line prompt gives Claude one decision (the goal) and leaves every other decision — color, interaction model, pedagogical scaffolding — to Claude's defaults; the three-file system (CLAUDE.md + DESIGN.md + PROJECT.md) moves those decisions from Claude's defaults to explicit human choices before a single line of code is generated.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = three-file system / human decision / yours; CRIMSON = one-line prompt / Claude default / not yours; never swap mid-film
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: NO Brutalist design system history, NO CSS custom properties tutorial, NO comparison with other design systems, NO full six-principle framework.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B09: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. The teacher typed one line: build me an interactive sorting algorithm simulator. The agentic loop ran for eight minutes. The result worked. It also was not theirs.

card.copy: Why the simulation that took 8 minutes to build wasn't theirs

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [TITLE] simulation in 8 minutes wasn't theirs

beats.narration_text: The simulator used Material Design's default palette: bright blue, white, gray. The teacher's class website used four earth tones. The interaction model was drag-and-drop. The teacher had wanted single-click stepping. The pedagogical scaffolding — what each color meant, why the algorithm slowed on certain inputs — was missing. The code worked. The lesson did not.
beats.scene_description: a browser window showing a sorting bar chart in generic blue-gray-white Material Design colors, editorial collage, desaturated, unfamiliar and corporate looking
beats.image_prompt: B02, a browser window showing a sorting algorithm bar chart visualization in generic Material Design blue-white-gray palette, bars of different heights, the interface looking corporate and unfamiliar, pinned to aged newsprint, editorial collage style, highly desaturated, cream and charcoal tones, no human faces
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL ai] generic Material Design sorting simulator

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [STILL ai] generic Material Design sorting simulator

beats.narration_text: Here is the question. A specific natural-language request should produce output matching the requester's intent. Here is the simulation where a clear-sounding request produced a technically correct result that was pedagogically wrong for this classroom. Why?

card.copy: 8 minutes. Working code. Not theirs. Why?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] the question on screen

beats.narration_text: One line of intent gives Claude one decision: the goal. Every other decision — color palette, interaction model, pedagogical scaffolding, what each element means to the student — Claude fills from defaults. The defaults are reasonable for some generic simulator. They are wrong for this simulator, in this classroom, for these students. The one-line prompt is not a brief. It is permission to invent.

production_viz.label: one line of intent; Claude fills every other decision with defaults
production_viz.mechanic: a single teal box at the top labeled 'intent: build a sorting simulator'; below it, four crimson boxes dropping in one at a time: 'color palette (Material Design default)', 'interaction model (drag-and-drop default)', 'pedagogical scaffolding (none)', 'typography (system default)'; each labeled 'Claude default'

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] intent box + four Claude-default boxes dropping in

beats.narration_text: The three-file system moves those decisions from Claude's defaults to explicit human choices — written down, before a single line of code is generated. CLAUDE.md is the technical constitution: stack, file structure, what Claude never touches. DESIGN.md is the visual constitution: six named colors, interaction vocabulary, explicit never-list. PROJECT.md Intent Layer is the pedagogical contract: what students should understand, what questions the simulation answers, what questions it refuses.

production_viz.label: three files: CLAUDE.md (technical) + DESIGN.md (visual) + PROJECT.md (pedagogical)
production_viz.mechanic: three teal file-card rows drawing in one at a time: 'CLAUDE.md — Technical Constitution: stack, file structure, never-touch'; 'DESIGN.md — Visual Constitution: six colors, interaction vocabulary, never-list'; 'PROJECT.md Layer 1 — Pedagogical Intent: what students understand, questions answered and refused'

beats.narration_text: The before-and-after. Without three files: one-line prompt produces bright blue, drag-and-drop, no pedagogical scaffolding — the teacher spends an hour correcting defaults. With three files: forty-five minutes of file-writing, then the build produces earth tones, single-click stepping, a comparison counter that makes O-n-squared visible — the teacher recognizes it as theirs.

production_viz.label: without three files: Claude defaults; with three files: teacher's decisions
production_viz.mechanic: two columns: left 'without three files' (crimson) — three rows: 'blue palette (default)', 'drag-and-drop (default)', 'no pedagogy (default)'; right 'with three files' (teal) — three rows: 'earth tones (DESIGN.md)', 'single-click stepping (DESIGN.md)', 'O(n^2) counter (PROJECT.md)'; a vs label between them

beats.narration_text: The DESIGN.md six-color rule. Six named variables: warm white, near-black, terracotta for the compared element, blue for the placed element, gray for axis lines, amber for the sorted region. If a sixth color seems necessary, escalate to the human. Do not invent. The rule is not about the number. It is about the boundary. A simulation with twenty colors is one where the design was made by Claude.

production_viz.label: six named colors: each has a defined role; a seventh escalates to the human
production_viz.mechanic: six color swatches drawing in with labels: warm white (background), near-black (text), terracotta (compared), blue (placed), gray (axis), amber (sorted); a seventh slot labeled 'escalate — do not invent' in crimson outline only

beats.narration_text: The practical move. The 45-minute test: if you cannot write all three files in 45 minutes, the simulation's scope is too large. Decompose. Build the smaller piece first. CLAUDE.md: fifteen minutes, mostly refined from slash-init. DESIGN.md: fifteen minutes — the six-color rule forces the decisions. PROJECT.md Intent Layer: fifteen minutes. Then the build. The simulation that follows is the size the framework can support.

production_viz.label: 45-minute test: three files in 15 minutes each; scope is right if it fits
production_viz.mechanic: a horizontal timeline: '0 min' to '45 min'; three teal segments: '0-15: CLAUDE.md', '15-30: DESIGN.md', '30-45: PROJECT.md Layer 1'; an arrow at 45 pointing right labeled 'then build'; a note 'over 45 min -> scope down'

beats.narration_text: Claude can generate the code. Claude cannot generate the lesson. The three files are the 45 minutes where you make the simulation yours before Claude starts.

card.copy: Three files. 45 minutes. Then it is yours.

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] endcard — three files 45 minutes then yours

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] endcard — three files 45 minutes then yours
